text: A forty-five-year-old woman came to our observation with acute abdominal pain. At the CT scan, presence of pneumoperitoneum, thickening of the sigma, free fluid in the pelvis. We decided to carry out an explorative laparoscopy.

The surgeon identifies purulent peritonitis and a perforation in the sigmoid colon. They begin the medial to lateral mobilization of the descending colon, sectioning the inferior mesenteric vein and detaching the mesocolon from the deep plane. The splenic flexure is not fully mobilized during this procedure.

Following the complete mobilization of the ascending colon, the surgeon identifies the left ureter. The mesosigma is mobilized while ensuring the artery is not sectioned at its origin, preserving vascularization.
The surgeon uses a stapler to section the colon at the promontory level, followed by the sectioning of the mesocolon adjacent to the colon.
The surgeon performs a mechanical circular anastomosis, detaching reinforcement points. No protective ileostomy or ghost ileostomy is created during this step.
The post-operative stay is regular. She discharged in five post-operative days.
